turns.content: I'm trying to make a POST request using reqwest but my code just hangs forever. I set up the client and added headers, yet nothing happens.

The issue is likely that you haven't actually awaited the async function or configured the timeout properly. When you call Client::new() in reqwest, it creates an async handle that needs to be used with .get(), .post(), etc., but those methods return a future that must be awaited before the request completes. Without awaiting, your program waits indefinitely because the HTTP handshake never finishes.
I know I need to await it, but what exactly should I do with the response? It seems like I'm missing something about how reqwest handles the connection pool.

The connection pool in reqwest is managed automatically by Client::new() and you don't need to configure it manually unless you have very specific performance needs. When you await a request like .post(), the library handles keeping connections alive between requests, which improves efficiency. However, if your code hangs, check that you're not forgetting the timeout configuration using Config::timeout() or letting the default infinite wait apply when the server doesn't respond.

Ah, I see now. So even though I'm setting headers correctly with Client::new(), the real problem was that I wasn't awaiting and also didn't set a timeout. How do I properly structure this so it doesn't hang?

You should create your client once, then use the async methods with proper timeouts. For example, you would call let response = reqwest::Client::new().post(url).header("Content-Type", "application/json").timeout(Duration::from_secs(30)).send(body).await?; This ensures that if the server takes too long to respond, your program will fail gracefully instead of hanging forever.
That makes sense. But what happens to the headers I set? Do they persist across multiple requests or do I need to redefine them every time?

Headers are set per-request when you call methods like .post() or .put(). You don't attach them once globally unless you use a builder pattern where you chain multiple configuration steps. Each request is independent, so if you want to send the same headers repeatedly, just include them in every request method call. The Client::new() instance itself manages the underlying TCP connections and pool, but individual HTTP requests are separate operations.
Okay, so I understand now. I was confusing the client initialization with per-request configuration. One more thing though - what if I want to send a PUT request instead?

PUT works exactly like POST in reqwest; you just change the method from .post() to .put(). The underlying Client::new() handles both methods identically regarding connection pooling and timeout. You can chain headers, set timeouts, and send data the same way: let response = client.put(url).header("X-Custom", "value").timeout(Duration::from_secs(60)).send(payload).await?;

I'm getting this error: 'the trait bound for reqwest::block::Client as std::convert::Into<reqwest::blocking::Client> is not satisfied'. I tried to use the blocking client but it seems incompatible. What went wrong with my setup?

Hey! That error happens because you're mixing async and blocking APIs incorrectly. The trait bound fails since reqwest::blocking::Client doesn't implement Into for the async version directly in your code context. You need to explicitly convert it or just use the async client from the start if you want to avoid that conversion step entirely.
Oh, I see. But how do I actually set up the blocking client properly? Do I just import reqwest::blocking and call Client::new()? Can I still use it for my proof-of-concept?

Yes, you can absolutely use the blocking client for your PoC. Just add `use reqwest::blocking;` at the top and then call `Client::new()` to get a standard blocking HTTP client instance. This gives you all the basic methods like GET/POST without needing async syntax.

Great, so if I want to do a POST request with custom headers on that blocking client, what's the exact way to chain those calls? I don't want to miss anything important like timeout or connection pooling settings.

You can call `.post(url).header(key, value)` to set headers, then `.timeout(duration)` for timeouts. For connection pooling, you usually configure it via the builder pattern before creating the client instance with `Client::builder().connect_timeout(...)`. Just make sure all these methods are chained correctly on the blocking Client object.
Wait a second! I thought connection pooling was only for async clients. Is it really possible to configure it with the blocking client too? That feels like a big win if true!

You're absolutely right to question that—it's a common misconception! Yes, you can configure connection pooling on the blocking client using `Client::builder().connect_timeout()` and other settings. It works exactly the same way as in async mode because it's built into the underlying crate implementation.
